Landscape and Urban Planning

Volume 261, September 2025, 105391

Urban community gardens foster positive human-avian interactions across an income gradient in San Francisco

Author links open overlay panel, , , , , , Nicole M. Ardoin e g, Gretchen C. Daily a b f g
https://doi-org.libproxy.berkeley.edu/10.1016/j.landurbplan.2025.105391Get rights and content
Under a Creative Commons license
Open access

Highlights

  • •
    Access to nature in cities, while critical to human well-being, is not equitably distributed.
  • •
    We investigated access to positive interactions with birds in 20 community gardens in San Francisco.
  • •
    Gardeners had positive attitudes towards birds overall, but different associations for each species.
  • •
    We found no relationship between metrics of access and garden income.
  • •
    Community gardens could foster equitable access to nature in cities.

Abstract

Access to nature in cities is critical to human well-being; however, it is not equitably distributed. In this study, we investigated a specific type of access to nature, access to positive interactions with urban birds. We explored whether these interactions are equitably distributed in 20 community gardens across an income gradient in the city of San Francisco, U.S.A. We used an interdisciplinary approach, combining ecological and social methods. To assess bird abundance and diversity, we conducted point counts. To understand gardener’s attitudes towards birds, we conducted online and in-person quantitative surveys. We found that gardeners had highly positive attitudes towards individual species and towards birds overall, despite slightly more negative attitudes towards two corvid species. Gardeners associated different ecological and aesthetic services and disservices with different species. Our models showed no significant relationship between our metrics of access and garden income. Instead, bird abundance and diversity was correlated with local and landscape environmental variables, only some of which were related to income. Our results suggest that the luxury effect does not exist for birds during the breeding season in community gardens in San Francisco. Investment in maintaining and growing community gardens could present a mutually beneficial solution for biodiversity and equitable nature access in the city.

Graphical abstract

  • Previous article in issue
  • Next article in issue

Keywords

Access to nature
Birds
Environmental justice
Luxury effect
Attitudes
Urban ecology

1. Introduction

Access to nature is considered a universal human right, with the United Nations Human Rights Council declaring that all people should have access to a “clean, healthy, and sustainable environment” (United Nations Human Rights Council, 2021). Urban nature provides key benefits including improved physical (Remme et al., 2021) and mental (Bratman et al., 2019) health to over half of the world’s population who live in cities (United Nations, 2018). Unfortunately, access to nature in cities is not evenly distributed, and historical and ongoing discrimination have led to disenfranchised communities living in proximity to less and more-degraded nature (Gerrish and Watkins, 2018, Grove et al., 2018, Locke et al., 2021, Watkins and Gerrish, 2018). Inequity in access to nature is an environmental justice issue that exacerbates gaps in health and wellbeing (Maas et al., 2006, Mitchell et al., 2015).
Although previous research has explored equity in access to greenspace in cities, there has been little focus on access to wildlife (Schell et al., 2020). Understanding access to wildlife may be key to ensuring that everyone in cities can enjoy the benefits that nature provides, given that wildlife is not equally distributed between neighborhoods and that urbanites value and benefit from coming into contact with wildlife (Cox et al., 2017, Cox and Gaston, 2016, Hammoud et al., 2022, Magle et al., 2021, Schell et al., 2020, Wood et al., 2024). In this study, we focus on birds, a well-studied taxon in urban ecology (Marzluff, 2017) and human-animal relationships (Echeverri et al., 2018, Muradian and Pascual, 2018), as a specific type of access to nature. We define access to nature after Langhans, et al. (2023) as “All possible means, including physical, spiritual, cultural, and mental, by which a person is able to benefit from any element of the biophysical system which includes flora, fauna, fungi, geological landforms and land and seascapes as a whole occurring across a range of scales and degrees of human presence” (p. 898).
Past studies have documented that people living in lower-income neighborhoods have access to less-diverse communities of wildlife than those living in higher-income neighborhoods. Researchers have observed a higher species richness in higher-income neighborhoods, a pattern known as the “luxury effect” (Leong et al., 2018). This pattern has mainly been observed for plants and birds, although there is mixed evidence for a luxury effect in other taxa such as mammals and insects (Chamberlain et al., 2020, Iuliano et al., 2017, Kuras et al., 2020, Magle et al., 2021). In addition, lower-income neighborhoods have a lower overall abundance of birds than higher-income neighborhoods (Wood & Esaian, 2020). These patterns may be driven by differing environmental characteristics between lower- and higher-income neighborhoods, such as the amount of impervious surface and canopy cover (Gerrish & Watkins, 2018) as well as vegetation diversity (Hope et al., 2003), which also affect urban bird communities (Chace and Walsh, 2006, Evans et al., 2009, Loss et al., 2009). Although differences in bird communities between lower- and higher-income neighborhoods are well-studied, how these differences affect the people living there and their experience of accessing nature remains vastly unknown.
Differences in bird communities between neighborhoods may represent an important element of urbanites’ access to nature due to the value people place on neighborhood birds. In the U.S.A., more urbanites engage with wildlife within one mile of their home (26%) than away from home (9%), and interact with birds more frequently than any other type of wildlife (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service 2023). Most urbanites notice birds in their neighborhoods and value interacting with them (Belaire et al., 2015, Clucas et al., 2015, Cox and Gaston, 2016). Bird-feeding, for example, is a popular global pastime and has been linked to feelings of increased wellbeing and emotional responses (Cox and Gaston, 2016, Dayer et al., 2019). Human-bird interactions more broadly have positive effects on wellbeing and mental health (Cox et al., 2017, Hammoud et al., 2022, McEwan et al., 2020).
However, people’s attitudes toward birds differ based on personal identity (Belaire et al., 2015, Clucas et al., 2015, Hedblom et al., 2017) in combination with the characteristics of the bird in question (Echeverri et al., 2019a, Goodness et al., 2016). These factors may influence the ecosystem services that urbanites perceive birds to provide. For example, Belaire et al. (2015) found that while Chicago residents generally appreciate the aesthetic value and ecological role of birds, they dis-prefer individual species that they perceive as nuisances. For these reasons, it is crucial to explicitly consider people’s attitudes when studying access to positive human-bird interactions.
We aimed to understand how access to positive interactions with bird species is distributed in community gardens across an income gradient in the city of San Francisco, California. We define community gardens as “open spaces which are managed and operated by members of the local community in which food or flowers are cultivated” (Guitart et al. 2012, p. 364). We explored the following questions:
1) How often do gardeners notice, and what are their attitudes towards and perceptions of birds?; 2) What is the relationship between neighborhood income and positive human-avian interactions? And 3) What are the environmental correlates of the avian abundance and diversity that gardeners are likely to encounter, and how are they related to neighborhood income?

2. Methods

Theories and methodology from both social and ecological sciences were used to evaluate access to positive human-bird interactions in community gardens (Fig. 1). Through gardener questionnaire surveys, we assessed whether gardeners noticed birds, what their attitudes were toward birds overall, and what their attitudes were toward 10 focal species (Q1). Using avian point counts at each garden, we quantified the presence and abundance of birds overall, as well as the presence and abundance of 10 focal species. We investigated how access to positive human-bird interactions in community gardens was related to neighborhood income to assess whether interactions were equitably distributed (Q2), and we examined the relationship between patterns in access and underlying environmental factors (Q3).

2.1. Area history and site selection

San Francisco, California, U.S.A., is an ideal location for a quasi-experimental study on the impact of income on access to nature. San Francisco is a compact city (127 km2) at the tip of a peninsula surrounded on three sides by seawater. The city has a steep income gradient, with census tract median household income ranging from USD $17,146 to >$250,000, and has a high cost of living relative to other similarly sized cities (Kirkeby, 2022, U.S. Census Bureau, 2022). The city has a high amount of greenspace, but it is not equitably distributed. For example, although not all parkland is greenspace, in San Francisco all residents live within a 10-minute walk of a park, and 21% of the city area is parkland (Trust for Public Land, 2022). However, neighborhoods composed primarily of residents of color and lower-income neighborhoods have 56% and 54% less park space, respectively, in comparison to predominantly white and higher-income neighborhoods (Trust for Public Land, 2022). San Francisco supports an array of habitats and features significant bird diversity: the city is part of the California floristic province biodiversity hotspot, is located near the largest estuary on the West Coast, and is a critical stopover point on the Pacific Flyway (Critical Ecosystem Partnership Fund, 2023).
Twenty community gardens and urban farms were selected as research sites (Fig. 2). These were chosen in neighborhoods across an income gradient using census tract-level five-year estimates of 2021 median household income from the American Community Survey (U.S. Census Bureau, 2022). The smallest gardens were excluded from our study to control for area effects on species richness. The 20 sites ranged in size from 405 to 7288 m2 and were spread across the city to capture geographic variation. All were located >500 m apart to ensure point count independence (Pennington & Blair, 2011, see supplemental Table C1 for site information). We excluded gardens and farms that primarily served youth, as prior studies have found youth and adults to have differing attitudes toward animals (Kellert, 1984a, Kupsala et al., 2016).

2.2. Ecological surveys

2.2.1. Quantifying bird diversity and abundance

To understand which bird species gardeners would be most likely to encounter, we performed bird surveys to quantify the species present and their relative abundance at each site. Specifically, we performed 15-minute 50 m fixed-radius point-counts from the center of each garden, recording all birds that we saw and heard. We also noted whether birds were in the garden, in the point-count radius, outside of the point-count radius, or flying overhead. For large gardens, the garden boundaries and point-count radius roughly coincided, but for small gardens the point-count radius typically extended beyond the garden. We performed point-counts from July-September 2022. Point-counts were performed from 9 AM-12 PM and 1 PM-4 PM to capture the hours of peak gardener activity (supplemental Fig. C1), with one count during each of those hours/site to control for time-of-day effects on detection (Ralph et al., 1995) for a total of 6 point-counts/site. While we acknowledge that bird activity is highest at dawn and dusk, we performed counts during the gardeners’ peak activity because we were interested in understanding gardener access to positive bird interactions.
We calculated three metrics with our point-count data: site species richness, site total abundance, and site abundance of each of 10 focal species (described below). We calculated these metrics using all birds we saw and heard during our point counts, including flyovers and those outside our point count radius (supplemental methods section B.1.) This represented a reasonable measurement of birds that a gardener could detect while in a garden. We also ran all income models with only birds detected within the point count radius, a conservative estimate of what gardeners might detect; this calculation method did not affect our results (supplemental results Appendix D).

2.2.2. Measuring site characteristics

We performed environmental surveys at each site to understand how garden characteristics influence avian communities. We measured the presence of native vegetation, presence and composition of hedgerows around the gardens, height of the tallest tree, percent land cover of different classes in the garden, and percent cover of different height classes in the garden, as proxies for vegetation characteristics that are known to influence bird diversity (Chace and Walsh, 2006, Evans et al., 2009). Crop diversity, particularly crops attractive to birds (e.g., with fruits and flowers), as well as bird attractors including perches, bird houses, bird baths and other water sources, and bird feeders were noted. Finally, we noted the presence of garden features meant to deter birds: model raptors, CDs and shiny plastic, and caged crops (supplemental Table C2).
We also used QGIS to measure seven landscape-scale variables that affect urban bird diversity: distance to the nearest road and distance to the nearest large (over 2 lane) road (after de Camargo Barbosa et al., 2020), effects of anthropogenic noise on urban birds); distance to the ocean (de Camargo Barbosa et al., 2020, distance to water affects species richness); distance to the nearest large greenspace (over 42 ha, after Donnelly & Marzluff, 2004); and percent canopy cover (Chace & Walsh, 2006), percent non-tree vegetation (after Chace and Walsh 2006, who review the importance of natural vegetation; pre-colonization San Francisco was primarily dune, grassland, and scrub habitats), and percent impervious surface (McKinney, 2002, Evans et al., 2018) within a 500 m buffer (supplemental methods section B.2). The 500 m scale was chosen after Pennington and Blair (2011), in which most bird species responded to urban landscape elements at a scale of 500 m and below. Percent canopy cover data at a 10 m resolution for 2020 was obtained from the California Forest Observatory (California Forest Observatory, 2020, most recent year available), percent non-tree vegetation at a 30 m resolution for 2022 from the National Land Cover Database (Multi-resolution Land Characteristics Consortium, 2022), and percent impervious surface data at a 30 m resolution for 2019 from the National Land Cover Database (Multi-resolution Land Characteristics Consortium, 2020, most recent year available). Data on large parks was obtained from the California Protected Areas Database (GreenInfo Network, 2024).

2.3. Questionnaire surveys

We surveyed gardeners at the 20 garden sites between October and December 2022 to investigate their attitudes and perceptions toward garden birds. We advertised the survey via recruitment posters at each garden site, word of mouth among gardeners, garden listservs, and in-person recruitment at the sites. Recruitment materials and surveys were available in English, Spanish, and Cantonese, the primary languages spoken by gardeners according to garden managers (personal communication, anonymous). We provided both online and in-person options for gardeners to complete the survey. We only analyzed data from gardeners aged 18 + who completed surveys (i.e., reached the end of the survey and submitted it, even if they did not answer all questions). All recruitment, survey, and data handling protocols were performed according to the standards of the Stanford University Institutional Review Board (IRB Protocol # 66353).
The survey questionnaire explored gardeners’ attitudes. In the first section, gardeners were asked a set of Likert-scale items about their relationship with birds, how often they noticed birds, and whether they would like to see more or fewer birds in the future.
Next, gardeners were also asked about their attitudes towards 10 focal species: American Crow (Corvus brachyrhynchos), American Robin (Turdus migratorius), Anna’s Hummingbird (Calypte anna), Black Phoebe (Sayornis nigricans), California Scrub Jay (Aphelocoma californica), House Finch (Haemorhous mexicanus), House Sparrow (Passer domesticus), Lesser Goldfinch (Spinus psaltria), Northern Mockingbird (Mimus polyglottos), and Red-tailed Hawk (Buteo jamaicensis). We chose these species because they are common in the gardens, unevenly distributed across the landscape, and have different physical and behavioral traits (including color, size, vocalization, non-native/native status, feeder usage, and diet guild). We used these criteria given that familiarity, abundance, and traits have been shown to influence people’s attitudes towards birds (Echeverri et al., 2019b, Goodness et al., 2016, Lišková and Frynta, 2013, Schuetz and Johnston, 2019). (For list of species traits, see supplementary Table C4.).
For each species, we presented gardeners with an illustration of the species, an audio clip of its vocalization (supplementary Table C6), and its common and Latin names. We asked gardeners whether they recognized each species. If they indicated that they did not, they would skip to the next bird; if they indicated that they did, they would write three words that they associated with the species. Species order was randomized among surveys.

2.4. Data analysis

All analyses were performed in QGIS ver. 3.16.3 and R ver. 4.4.1 using the packages abdiv (Bittinger, 2020), biscale (Prener & Grossenbacher, 2022), car (Fox & Weisberg, 2019), corrplot (Wei & Simko, 2021), ggeffects (Lüdecke, 2018), ggpubr (Kassambara, 2023), nlme (Pinheiro et al., 2023), sf (Pebesma & Bivand, 2023), tidyverse (Wickham et al., 2019), and wordcloud (Fellows, 2025). The full reproducible code is available at https://github.com/kelley-langhans/SFGardenBirds.

2.4.1. Calculating neighborhood income

We used the income of the neighborhood surrounding the garden as a measure of site income because we wanted to know how characteristics of each gardener’s neighborhood affected their access to nature. The average gardener at our sites lived a median of 1,627 m distance away from their garden, so we calculated garden neighborhood income as the spatially weighted average of median household income of census tracts within a 1,627 m radius buffer (an area of 8,295,768 m2) around the garden (supplemental Fig. C3). We derived census median household income data from the American Community Survey 5-year estimate for 2021, with missing data filled in (U.S. Census Bureau, 2022). From the calculations, we excluded census tracts with fewer than 400 households (n = 4), which represented parks and industrial areas. (For more detail, see supplemental methods section B.3).

2.4.2. Species sentiment analysis and ecosystem services

We assessed gardener attitudes toward our 10 focal species using a sentiment analysis (Echeverri et al., 2017). Beginning with the list of words that gardeners associated with each species, we coded each word as either positive, negative, or neutral (Table C7; for a full list of coded words, see supplemental Tables C8-10. For details, see supplemental methods section B.4.). We also coded words to assess the ecological and aesthetic ecosystem services and disservices that gardeners perceived each species to provide. Ecological services included mentions of species’ roles in pollination, scavenging, seed dispersal, and pest control, and mentions of the species helping people or gardens; ecological disservices included mentions of negative impacts of the species on gardens. Aesthetic services included positive mentions of the species’ appearance or sound or mentions of enjoying encountering the species, while aesthetic disservices were the inverse (Table C7; for full list of coded words, see supp Tables C11-14; for details, see supplemental methods section B.5.). Coding for all species was initially performed by a single individual and all codes were subsequently verified by a second individual.
Species were assigned a sentiment score Eq. (1):Sentiment score=(Σpositive words-Σnegative words)(Σpositive words+Σnegative words)As some species were recognized more frequently than others, we further calculated a weighted sentiment score (Eq 2):Weighted sentiment score=sentiment score∗%of gardeners who recognized speciesTo examine gardener perceptions of ecosystem services and disservices, the percentage of all words associated with each species that fell into each of the four categories was calculated and compared to the species’ unweighted sentiment score.

2.4.3. Modeling relationships between richness, abundance, and income

We ran generalized linear regression models (GLMs) with a negative binomial distribution to explore the relationship between garden neighborhood income and avian species richness and abundance at the sites. In these models, income was an explanatory variable, and richness and abundance were response variables. As our sites differed during surveys in total number of observers and average temperature, both of which we found to predict species richness and abundance in individual point count surveys, we also included those as covariates (supplemental methods section B.6). We ran a second set of models with a quadratic income term. We performed additional tests comparing the abundance of subsets of synanthropic, shrub-affiliated, and tree-affiliated species with garden neighborhood income to determine whether patterns held true for synanthropic and non-synanthropic species (supplemental methods section B.7).

2.4.4. Modeling relationships between species access and income

For each site, we created a species access metric as (Eq 3):Species Access Metric=Σspecies'(weighted sentiment score∗site abundance)With this metric, sites with more individuals of noticeable species that generally engender positive sentiments had high species access scores. To further explore whether a relationship existed between garden neighborhood income and gardener contact with preferred and recognized birds, we ran a linear regression model on our 20 sites with income as an explanatory variable, total number of observers and average temperature as covariates, and our species access metric as the predicted variable. We also ran a second version of model with a quadratic income term.

2.4.5. Modeling relationships between richness, abundance, and site characteristics

We investigated the relationships between local and landscape-level garden characteristics and bird richness and abundance to explore potential underlying ecological mechanisms.
For our models, in order to reduce the number of variables and address collinearity, we summarized all local-level vegetation data (see section 2.2) using a Principal Components Analysis (PCA). PC1 (28% variation) had strong positive loadings for ground (as opposed to vertical structure), bare ground, and impervious surface, and strong negative loadings for most types of vegetation. PC2 (21% variation) had strong positive loadings for non-crop non-native vegetation and forbs, and strong negative loadings for all crop types and shrubs (supplemental Figs. C21 & 22, Table C23).
In our initial models, we included only variables for which we had ecological hypotheses (see supplemental Table C24). Our full initial set of candidate variables included the first four local vegetation principle components axes, presence of bird detractors, presence of water, number of posts/perches, garden area, 500 m canopy cover, 500 m non-tree vegetation, 500 m impervious surface, distance to the ocean, distance to the closest major road, distance to the closest minor road, distance to the closest large greenspace, and finally number of observers and average temperature as control variables. Area and distance-based environmental variables were log-transformed where necessary. We eliminated collinear variables and then used stepwise AIC selection (or F-test-based selection, where AIC was not appropriate) to choose the variables to include in our final models. Our final species richness model included PC1, detractors, and distance to closest major road, and our final abundance model included PC1, PC2, posts, detractors, distance to major road, and canopy cover. We ran GLMs with a Poisson distribution, correcting for overdispersion in the abundance model by using a quasi-GLM model, with variance given by φ × μ, where μ is the mean and φ is the dispersion parameter. We did not use a hierarchical model such as an N-mixture model that could account for imperfect detection because our data structure did not fit the model (For details, see supplemental methods B.8.). Instead, we ran our models using total species richness and abundance per site, as detected across the 6 surveys.
To test for indirect effects of income on bird species richness and abundance mediated by environmental variables, we calculated Pearson correlations between any significant environmental variables from these models and site income.

3. Results

3.1. Point count surveys

Across our 120 surveys, we observed 3,654 birds in total, with a mean of 183 birds/garden and a range of 108 to 337. We also observed 49 species in total, with a mean of 19 species/garden and a range of 11 to 27. Our 10 focal species were all in the top 25 most abundant species we observed, with three of them in the top five (House Finch [1], Anna’s Hummingbird [3], House Sparrow [4]) (supplemental Table C15). Our focal species differed in how common they were at our 20 garden sites: while some species (House Finch, Anna’s Hummingbird) were present at all 20 gardens, the least common (Northern Mockingbird) was present at only five (supplemental Table C16).

3.2. Gardener surveys

In total, we collected survey responses from 165 adult gardeners at our 20 sites. Estimated response rates per garden ranged from 3-50% (supplemental Table C17). Respondents were primarily female (63%), white (71%), college-educated (85%), and 55 + years of age (55%) (supplemental Table C18). We received responses from gardeners across a broad income range: 47% of respondents qualified as low income, 13% as low/moderate income, and 20% as high income (based on income thresholds for four-person households from the California Department of Housing and Community Development, (Kirkeby, 2022)); 20% of respondents did not report their income. With regard to gardening tenure, 75% had been at the garden for over a year, and 44% for five or more years. Additionally, over 91% of respondents reported living in the Bay Area for 10 or more years. Most respondents did not report spending much time learning about birds, did not consider themselves to be birders, and did not consider themselves knowledgeable about birds (supplemental Table C19).

3.3. Gardener attitudes toward birds overall

Overall, gardeners held positive attitudes toward birds. When asked why they value nature in their garden, 20% explicitly mentioned birds, and 83% listed birds when asked more specifically about which animals they enjoyed seeing in the garden. Most gardeners agreed that they enjoyed seeing and hearing birds; encountering birds in the garden was important to them; encountering birds in the garden made them feel connected to nature; birds played an important ecological role in the garden; and birds were beneficial to the plants they were growing in the garden. The majority also disagreed that birds in the garden were pests (Fig. 3). Moreover, when asked whether they would like to see more or fewer birds in the garden, 81% of gardeners answered that they would like to see more birds in the garden; the remaining 19% said they would like to see the same amount. Fewer than 6% of respondents said that they rarely or never noticed birds in the garden.

3.4. Gardener attitudes toward individual species

In general, gardeners displayed overwhelmingly positive attitudes toward all 10 focal species (supplemental Figs. C4-7). Sentiment analysis results illuminated some differences in attitudes and perceptions among species. Of the 2,911 words and phrases gardeners used, we coded 1,032 as positive, 307 as negative, and 1,572 as neutral (supplemental Tables C8-10). Individual species differed in the number of positive and negative words associated with them; some, like the Anna’s Hummingbird, had far more positive associations, while others, like the American Crow, had more negative associations (Fig. 4 A, B, C, supplemental Figs. C8-15).
When species sentiment scores were weighted by recognition, the rankings of the species changed, such that highly recognized birds like the Red-tailed Hawk and Anna’s Hummingbird were higher ranked, while the Black Phoebe, toward which gardeners had only positive sentiments but few recognized, was ranked lowest (Fig. 4 D). There was no relationship between species abundance and the percentage of gardeners who recognized each species (Fig. C16).

3.5. Perceived services of individual species

The relative importance of ecological and aesthetic services and disservices changed between species (Fig. 4 E). Birds with the lowest sentiment scores were associated with relatively more disservices than services, and relatively more aesthetic than ecological disservices. Birds with higher sentiment scores were associated with relatively more services than disservices, but the relative importance of ecological and aesthetic services differed between species. While some species, like the Red-tailed Hawk, had a higher percentage of words coded as ecological than aesthetic services, other species, like the Lesser Goldfinch, had a higher percentage of aesthetic than ecological service words.

3.6. Neighborhood income and nature access

We did not find any significant relationship between garden neighborhood income and bird abundance (β = -1.01e-6, p-value = 0.71), species richness (β = -1.12e-7, p-value = 0.96), or our species access metric (β = 3.60e-5, p-value = 0.87) (Fig. 5, Table 1, supplemental Figs. C17, 19-20). Results were also not significant when we calculated avian metrics using conservative point counts (supplemental results D.1, supplemental Table C20), or when we included a quadratic income term (supplemental Table C21). The lack of significant relationships between abundance and income held when species were separated into synanthropes and habitat-affiliated guilds (supplemental results D.2, supplemental Table C22 and Fig. C18).

3.7. Environmental correlates of bird diversity in gardens

In the optimal model for species richness using environmental variables, richness was positively associated with distance to a large road and negatively associated with PC1 for site local vegetation. The best-fit model for avian abundance using environmental variables showed positive associations with detractors, number of posts, PC2 for site local vegetation, and distance to a large road. It also had negative associations with PC1 for site local vegetation and percent canopy at 500 m (Fig. 5, Table 2, Fig. C19). Among these variables, only distance to large roads and site local vegetation PC1 were significantly correlated with income (Pearson correlation = -0.47, p-value = -0.12; Pearson correlation = -0.44, p-value = 0.051; respectively) (Fig. 5, supplemental Table C25).

4. Discussion

This study demonstrates that gardeners in San Francisco generally notice and hold positive attitudes toward birds, but variations exist in their perceptions of individual species. These differences manifest in the ecological and aesthetic services and disservices attributed to each species. Surprisingly, no significant correlation between neighborhood income and positive human-bird interactions in community gardens was found. Instead, bird abundance and diversity were more influenced by local and landscape characteristics unrelated to income, indicating equitable distribution of these interactions across neighborhoods.
Gardeners held positive attitudes toward the bird community overall, suggesting that encountering birds may be an important way of accessing nature. Gardeners generally enjoyed seeing and hearing birds, enjoyed the way encountering birds made them feel, and felt that birds were important to the ecosystem and the garden. These reflect commonly held values of urban Americans toward wildlife, including the naturalistic, ecologistic, aesthetic, and utilitarian values identified by Kellert (Kellert, 1984b).
However, gardeners differed in how much they noticed, their sentiments towards, and the ecosystem services and disservices that they associated with individual species, suggesting that each species contributes differently toward access to positive human-bird interactions (Fig. 1 Box 1). Our sentiment analysis found more negative associations with corvids than other species, corroborating previous findings that people have different attitudes toward different bird species (Clucas et al., 2015, Cox and Gaston, 2015, Echeverri et al., 2019b), and that urban people in general do not prefer corvids (Belaire et al., 2015, Clucas et al., 2015, Cox and Gaston, 2015). However, even corvids were more controversial than universally disliked, with gardeners also appreciating them for being “smart” and “beautiful.” Colorful birds, especially blue and yellow species, and frugivores and nectivores tend to be more popular among general public as well as farmers and urbanites in particular as compared to insectivores, scavengers, and carnivores (Echeverri et al., 2019b, Lišková and Frynta, 2013). However, we found the bird with the highest unweighted sentiment score in our study (the Black Phoebe) was a black insectivore, the singular blue bird (the California Scrub Jay) had the second lowest sentiment score, and the raptor (the Red-tailed Hawk) had a high sentiment score. This indicates that the attitudes birds engender may depend on a suite of traits and perceived services and disservices (for example, the California Scurb Jay’s blue-ness may be cancelled out by its scavenger behavior and loud call). Interestingly, while species in our study with lower sentiment scores were indeed associated with more ecosystem disservices, they had relatively more aesthetic than ecological disservices. This suggests that people’s dislike of certain species may be primarily aesthetically-driven, even in the garden context where certain species (i.e. granivores, frugivores) could damage crops. We also found that individual species differed in the relative importance of aesthetic and ecological services, similar to the findings of Belaire et al. (2015) that urbanites associate a variety of services with birds. As positive human-bird interactions rely partially on the level of people’s familiarity with birds, campaigns to increase awareness of the ecological services species provide could also increase access to positive human-bird interactions in gardens.
Contrary to the expected “luxury effect,” we did not observe a significant relationship between avian abundance, species richness, and neighborhood income. Unlike in Wood et al. (2024), where synanthropic bird species showed a negative association with luxury and tree- and shrub-specialists a positive association, the lack of relationship remained when we analyzed the abundance of a subset of species in each guild. Meta-analyses have shown that the luxury effect is stronger in sparse, tropical, and/or arid cities, in plants rather than animals, and in residential as compared to public lands (Chamberlain et al., 2020, Kuras et al., 2020). Our study took place in a dense, temperate city with a non-arid Mediterranean climate and was focused on birds, factors which may have weakened the relationship between socio-economic status and biodiversity. The theory behind the luxury effect posits that residents of higher income neighborhoods will have greater resources to invest in more diverse plant communities, which will in turn attract more diverse animal communities (Hope et al., 2003, Leong et al., 2018). While the community gardens we worked with were primarily located on public lands (city-owned, national park, school district-owned), they represent a particular mix of public and private investment in resources. Some gardens were entirely communally-planted and maintained by the city or non-profit organizations, while many others had a mix of plots planted by individuals and communally-planted lands. This input of external resources into community gardens could also disrupt the link between neighborhood income and plant, and therefore bird, biodiversity, although other have shown that luxury does impact plant communities in community gardens (Clarke and Jenerette, 2015, Iuliano et al., 2017, Philpott et al., 2023). The impact of present-day neighborhood income on biodiversity may be diluted or complicated by other complex social-environmental relationships in cities, such as cultural planting preferences, development policies, and the legacy effect of historic disinvestment in neighborhoods such as redlining (Magle et al., 2021, Wood et al., 2024). Alternatively, community gardens could act as “refugia”, providing habitat for bird species in lower-income otherwise habitat-poor neighborhoods, as was shown to be the case in public parks in Los Angeles, California (Vasquez & Wood, 2022). Future studies could explore how human/bird interactions in other contexts, such as on streets or around peoples’ homes, are distributed across an income gradient.
Instead of income, other local and landscape-scale factors shaped bird communities in gardens. We had originally hypothesized that neighborhood income would influence the landscape and garden level environment, which would in turn influence bird communities (see supplemental Table C24 for full set of hypotheses). Instead, we found that only some of the factors that we measured—canopy cover and distance to large roads at the landscape level, and vegetation PC1 (bare ground and impervious surface vs. vegetation), vegetation PC2 (non-crop non-native vegetation and forbs vs. crops and shrubs), presence of detractors, and number of posts at the garden level—predicted species richness and abundance in community gardens. Although area of green space has been shown to be one of the strongest predictors of urban bird biodiversity (Callaghan et al., 2018), including in community gardens (Mayorga et al., 2020), garden area was not in our best fit models as it was highly collinear with habitat features of the gardens themselves.
Additionally, contrary to our hypotheses, only some predictors of bird abundance and diversity were significantly correlated with income. While higher-income gardens had lower scores on vegetation PC1 (less bare ground and impervious surface and more vegetation), which positively predicted bird abundance and diversity, they were also closer to large roads, which negatively predicted bird abundance and diversity. The first of these could be explained by the luxury effect theory: that higher-income gardens have more resources to invest in planting and maintaining vegetative cover, especially outside of their own plots. Indeed, other studies have found a relationship between socio-economic status and flowering and ornamental plants in community gardens (Clarke and Jenerette, 2015, Iuliano et al., 2017, Philpott et al., 2023). The second could be because higher land costs in higher-income neighborhoods meant that city and non-profit entities could only afford to acquire less desirable land closer to large roads for community gardens. Regardless, the competing and neutral relationships between the predictors of increased bird abundance and diversity and income may further explain why we did not detect a luxury effect in our study.
Our study had several limitations, and future socio-ecological studies could further elucidate patterns in positive human/bird interactions in cities. Firstly, although we attempted to understand how bird biodiversity was related to income and mediated by environmental factors, there may be important social and environmental drivers of biodiversity that we did take into account, such as the legacy effect (Schell et al., 2020). Secondly, we are aware of certain gardener demographic groups that were underrepresented in our surveys, which may have biased our results as attitudes towards birds differ based on demographics (Clucas et al., 2015, Belaire et al., 2015, Hedblom et al., 2017). Thirdly, our bird and human surveys were seasonal and non-overlapping, with bird surveys in the summer and human surveys in the fall/winter. Overlapping the surveys temporally could more accurately reflect gardeners’ attitudes towards the bird communities they are experiencing. Although we timed our bird surveys to match periods of human activity in the gardens, this may have affected bird detection, especially of rare species. Additionally, the luxury effect may change seasonally in San Francisco, with wintering birds making use of neighborhoods differently than migrating and breeding birds. Future studies could explore patterns across seasons to track changes in bird/environment and bird/human relationships. Finally, we only explored a single axis of power, income, in our analysis. A richer understanding of equitable access to positive human/bird interactions in cities could be gained by considering a more complex understanding of how power shapes biodiversity and who has access to it (Poulton Kamakura et al., 2024), including additional axes, such as race, class, and sexuality.
These findings add to the literature of community gardens as important resources in the urban environment, serving not only sources of food security, health benefits, and education but also as critical components of creating a sense of place, environmental justice, community organization, and resistance against gentrification. Despite their value, community gardens are often transient spaces marked by insecure land tenure, rising and falling with the capacity of community organizers to maintain and advocate for them (Glowa et al., 2019, Lawson, 2005). Policies that support the maintenance of existing community gardens, as well as the creation of new gardens, would help meet the demand for this type of greenspace demonstrated by years-long waitlists to join gardens in San Francisco. Additionally, the design of bird-friendly gardens through incorporating hedgerows, birdbaths, trees, and seed-bearing, fruiting, and flowering plants would further support both bird diversity and nature access. Such interventions, designed and enacted through the principles of recognitional, procedural, and distributional justice, would provide the opportunity to help increase broader and more equitable access to nature in the city.

5. Conclusions

Our work has shown that people value interacting with birds in San Francisco’s community gardens. These findings support an expanded definition of access to nature that encompasses many kinds of positive human-nature interactions and goes beyond simply encountering greenspace (Langhans et al., 2023). Yet, it is important to acknowledge the multiplicity of values associated with birds, how those values differ across people and based on the species in question. Human-nature relationships are complex and context-dependent, and while some aspects of them can be generalized, they should be measured explicitly to uphold the value of recognitional justice. Our work has also shown that gardeners generally like birds, although gardeners may not recognize all of them. The connection to nature and enjoyment that people gain from human-bird interactions may depend on their familiarity with species (Cox and Gaston, 2015, Echeverri et al., 2017), which suggests that access to nature via positive human/bird interactions could be enhanced through environmental education efforts that raise awareness and address negative sentiments toward certain species.
Our research supports the proposed view that community gardens act as win/win spaces for people and biodiversity, and are greenspaces that can equitably increase access to nature. Although these relatively compact greenspaces may not meet the needs of all bird species or be ecological source habitats that support birds through all lifecycle phases, they can provide useful supplemental habitat for many species rich in food resources. For people, the benefits that we identify in this study are just a few among the myriad ecosystem services that community gardens provide to urbanites (Jha et al., 2023), marking these greenspaces as truly multifunctional in the ways that they can support the needs of the human community as well as the broader ecosystem.

Positionality statement

We are ornithologists, ecologists, and conservation social scientists who draw on anthropocentric conceptions of human-nature relationships in this paper. Several of us grew up in and most of us currently reside in the San Francisco Bay Area. All of us live in or have lived in urban areas across an income gradient. Several of us are gardeners, many of us are birders, and we all self-identify as environmentalists. Those of us who performed most of the fieldwork are White and Asian women whose primary language is English, which likely influenced who was willing to speak with us and what they shared about their relationship with their environment. Our team includes female and nonbinary; White, Asian, and Latina; and immigrant and settler-colonial co-authors. All our identities influenced how we conceptualized and measured access to nature in our study and interpreted our data.

Author contributions

KEL conceptualized the study and refined the design with AE, ON, MX, and GCD. KEL designed the gardener survey with input from AE, MC, and NMA. KEL and MX carried out the ecological fieldwork, and KEL carried out the gardener survey fieldwork. KEL led data analysis assisted by MX and MLP, with input from AE, MC, ON, NMA, and GCD. KEL wrote the manuscript assisted by MX, and all authors edited the manuscript. GCD and NMA supervised the study.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Kelley E. Langhans: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Alejandra Echeverri: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Maya Xu: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Meggie Callahan: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Mei Li Palmeri: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Oliver Nguyen: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Nicole M. Ardoin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Gretchen C. Daily: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

Funding

This research was supported by the Winslow Foundation and the Reid and Polly Anderson Fund. KEL was supported by the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship and the Ward Wilson Woods, Jr., Fellowship in Environmental Studies.

Acknowledgments

This research was conducted on the ancestral lands of the Muwkema Ohlone, and we express deep gratitude for their enduring stewardship that has shaped the ecosystems we study. Special thanks to Kathi Baxter, Will Lyons, and Mei Ling Hui from the San Francisco Department of Recreation and Parks Community Garden Program and garden leaders Lance Mellon, Ted Fang, Matt Satlak, and Wendy Harris for granting research permission and facilitating connections with gardeners. We are indebted to the generosity of gardeners at our 20 sites—Adam Rogers, Alemany Farm, Arlington, Crags Court, Dogpatch/Miller Memorial, Florence Fang, Fort Mason, Golden Gate Park CommUNITY, In Chan Kaajal, Koshland Park, La Grande, Michaelangelo, Potrero del Sol, Potrero Hill, Sunset, Visitación Valley Greenway, and White Crane Springs—whose welcoming spirit, participation in surveys, and collaboration made this study possible.
Special recognition goes to Ruby Gates and Alexa Pollock for their contributions to gardener survey fieldwork, and to Tuesday Utz, Lexi Triantis, and Valeria Alejandra Vega Yanez for their assistance in survey coding and outreach materials.

Data availability

De-identified data and code used in these analyses are available in the following GitHub repository: https://github.com/kelley-langhans/SFGardenBirds. Survey data containing zip codes is identifiable and has not been made publically available. For questions about zip-code related analyses, please contact the corresponding author.
